When the inspection train finally curved into the northern cut, having spent the better part of an afternoon crawling over bridges whose bolts dated from three administrations and through settlements where the station signs had faded to the color of old tea, Elena rose from the stiff red seat beside the window, pressed her palm to the glass to cut the glare, and tried to determine, from the scattered roofs, the narrow schoolyard, the half-collapsed grain elevator, and the line of cottonwoods bending toward the tracks, whether the town ahead was the one her father had described in such dense, affectionate detail that she had always imagined it as a place beyond geography, the kind of place that could only exist in the long, embroidered sentences of a person who feared that if he paused for breath the whole remembered world might slip out through the space created by silence.

She told herself, as the engine groaned and the conductor's keys rattled somewhere behind her, that memory is not improved by haste, and yet she also knew, because she had been a child in that household and later the keeper of its papers, that he had learned to speak in braided clauses for a reason: a short sentence allows an ending to arrive too early, whereas a long one can hold another face, another lane, another weather report, another tiny correction -- no, not Tuesday, Wednesday after rain -- and by holding them it delays the loneliness that begins the instant a story admits that its world is over.

So she remained standing, balancing with one knee against the seat, while the train slowed, and she tried not to decide too quickly whether the faded mural on the brick warehouse was hopeful or exhausted, whether the single bicyclist lifting one hand toward the crossing gate meant welcome or mere habit, whether the smell drifting in through the cracked seal of the window was cut hay or river mud, because once she chose one interpretation the others would fall away, and she wanted, for a few more seconds, to inhabit the larger sentence in which all of them were still possible.
